records.content:
Water flows out of a large tank and through the pipeline as shown in Figure 2.

a. Construct the energy grade line and hydraulic grade line for the pipe, labelling the relevant quantities on your diagram.

b. Using your solution to part (a), calculate the pressure and velocity within the 200mm diameter section of the pipe.

c. State any assumptions you have made within your answers to (a) and (b)

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/14.4.png")
img



Answer


b) \;43.53 \; kPa \;,\; 3.32 \; m/s \; 

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Click here for a video explaining the problem

Question 4 (a)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The EGL (Energy Grade Line) is the total energy at each point along the flow. This includes the kinetic energy and the potential energy. Considering there is no friction loss in this exercise, the EGL should be a horizontal line, strarting from the water surface to the exit of the pipeline.EGL= \frac{p}{\gamma}+\frac{V^2}{2g} +z

The HGL (Hydraulic Grade Line) represents the sum of the pressure head and the elevation head. At the reservoir, the HGL will be at the same level of the water surface, but as the water flows down the pipe, the HGL will drop at each point due to the increasing velocity. At the pipe exit, the pressure head becomes zero, so the HGL will end at the same level as the exit of the pipelineHGL= \frac{p}{\gamma}+z

Solution

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/14.4S.png")
img



Question 4 (b)

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/14.4T.png")
img



 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The velocity can be calculated with the Toricelli equation in the pipe exit.V_3=\sqrt (2gh)

Once the velocity at the exit is calculated, the discharge can be determined with:Q_3=V_3*A_3

Once the discharge is calculated, the velocity in the 200 mm diameter pipe can be determined by using the conservation of massQ_1=Q_2=Q_3\\
Q_3=V_2*A_2\\
V_2=\frac{Q_3}{A_2}\\
V_2=\frac{Q_34}{\pi D^2}

Once the velocity in this pipe is calculated, the Bernoulli equation can be determine between the surface of the tank and that pipe\frac{p_{atm}}{\gamma}+\frac{V_1^2}{2g} +z_1=\frac{p_2}{\gamma}+\frac{V_2^2}{2g} +z_2

The following assumptions can be madep_{atm}=0 Pa\\
z_2= 0 m\\
V_1= 0 m/s

Using this, the Bernoulli equation for this exercise is:z_1=\frac{p_2}{\gamma}+\frac{V_2^2}{2g}

Solution

%%render long 2 
z_1= 5 #m 
D_1= 200 #mm 
D_2= 100 #mm
D_3= 100#mm 
z_t= 9 #m

V_3= sqrt (2*g*z_t) #m/s
Q_3= V_3*pi*(D_3/1000)**2/4 #m3/s

V_2= Q_3*4/(pi*(D_1/1000)**2) #m/s

p_2= ((z_1-V_2**2/(2*g))*rho_water*g)/1000 #kPa



Question 4 (c)

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->Incompressible fluid, no losses in pipe, diameter of free discharge is equal to pipe diameter.

records.content:
An object is placed in an open channel such that a stagnation point (zero velocity) is created on the leading edge of the object. You may assume that the density of water is equal to 1000 kg/m3 for this problem.

a. If the water in the channel is flowing at a velocity of 2 m/s, calculate the dynamic pressure at the stagnation point.

b. If the object is replaced by a pitot tube, which also creates a stagnation point within the same flow, calculate the height to which water would rise in the pitot tube.

c. A short time later, the velocity in the open channel has changed. The water level in the pitot tube is now 150 mm. Calculate the new velocity in the channel.

d. Explain which line is represented by the water level in the pitot tube, and which line is represented by the water level in the channel itself.

Answer


a) \;2 \; kPa


b) \;h= \; 0.204\; m


c) \;1.72 \; m/s

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Click here for a video explaining the problem

Question 5 (a)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Apply the Bernoulli equation between two points: point 1 located within the flow, and point 2 at the stagnation point:\frac{p_{1}}{\gamma}+\frac{V_1^2}{2g} +z_1=\frac{p_2}{\gamma}+\frac{V_2^2}{2g} +z_2

Remember that the velocity will be zero at the stagnation point.

Solution

%%render long 1 
V_1=0 #m/s
V_2= 2 #m/s

Delta_p= ((V_2**2/(2*g))*rho_water*g)/1000 #kPa



Question 5 (b)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The height to which water would rise in the pitot tube can be solved from the following equation:\Delta p=\rho gh

The height of water in the pitot tube above the water surface in the channel will be equal to the velocity head in the channel.

Solution

%%render long 3 
h= Delta_p*1000/(rho_water*g) #m



Question 5 (c)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Again, you can apply the Bernoulli equation between a point in the flow and the stagnation point, or simply recognise that the water level in the pitot tube above the water surface in the channel will represent the velocity head.

Solution

%%render long 2 
h=150/1000 #m
V= sqrt(2*g*h) #m/s



Question 5 (d)

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->

The water level in the pitot tube represents the EGL, while the water level in the channel represents the HGL.

records.content:
Water flows through a pipe (diameter of 50 mm). A pitot tube placed in the flow measures a water height of 150 mm relative to the centre of the pipe, and a piezometer at the side of the pipe measures a water height of 90 mm relative to the
centre of the pipe.

a. Determine the average velocity of the water flowing in the pipe.

b. Calculate the dynamic pressure at the stagnation point of the pitot tube.

c. Draw the EGL and HGL for this problem.

Answer


a) \;1.08 \; m/s


b) \;589 \; Pa

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Click here for a video explaining the problem

Question 6 (a)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The piezometer measures the pressure head component of the Bernoulli equation along with the elevation head (𝑧). The Pitot tube measures the pressure head, the velocity head, and the elevation head (z). This means the Pitot tube measures the Energy Grade Line (EGL) while the piezometer measures the Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL).

Considering the equation for HGL:HGL= \frac{p}{\gamma} + z

And the equation for EGL:EGL= \frac{p}{\gamma} + \frac{V^2}{2g}+ z

The difference between the lines is equal to the velocity head.

Solution

%%render long 2 
D= 50 #mm
h_pit=150#mm
h_p=90 #mm 

V= sqrt (2*g*((h_pit/1000)-h_p/1000)) #m/s



Question 6 (b)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The dynamic pressure (\Delta p) can be calculated using the formula:\Delta p= \frac{\rho V^2}{2}

Solution

%%render long 0
Delta_p= rho_water*V**2/2 #Pa



Question 6 (c)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The piezometer measures the pressure head component of the Bernoulli equation along with the elevation head (𝑧). The Pitot tube measures the pressure head, the velocity head, and the elevation head (z). This means the Pitot tube measures the Energy Grade Line (EGL) while the piezometer measures the Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL).

Considering the equation for HGL:HGL= \frac{p}{\gamma} + z

And the equation for EGL:EGL= \frac{p}{\gamma} + \frac{V^2}{2g}+ z

We know that the Energy Grade Line (EGL) is 0.15 m and the Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL) is 0.09 m. Assuming there are no friction losses, both lines will be horizontal.

Solution

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/14.6.png")
img

records.content:
Water flows through the pipe shown in the figure with a velocity of 3 m/s (in the 200 mm section of the pipe).

a. If the pressure in the 200 mm section of the pipe is 350 kPa, calculate the pressures in the other two sections of pipe.

b. Draw the EGL and HGL for this problem.

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/14.7.png")
img



Answer


a) \;319.78 \; kPa\;,\;178.72\; kPa

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Click here for a video explaining the problem

Question 7 (a)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The pressure in the 120 mm section can be calculated using the Bernoulli equation between the 200 mm and the 120 mm sections:\frac{p_{1}}{\gamma}+\frac{V_1^2}{2g} +z_1=\frac{p_2}{\gamma}+\frac{V_2^2}{2g} +z_2

The pressure in the 80 mm section can be calculated using the Bernoulli equation between the 120 mm and the 80 mm sections:\frac{p_{2}}{\gamma}+\frac{V_2^2}{2g} +z_2=\frac{p_3}{\gamma}+\frac{V_3^2}{2g} +z_3

As the pipe is horizontal, z_1=z_2, and V_2 and V_2 can be calculated considering conservation of mass:V= \frac{Q}{A}

Solution

%%render long 2
V_1= 3 #m/s
D_1= 200/1000 #m
D_2= 120/1000 #m
D_3= 80/1000 #m
p_1=350*1000 #Pa

Q= V_1*(pi*D_1**2/4) #m3/s
V_2= Q/(pi*D_2**2/4) #m/s
p_2= (p_1/(rho_water*g)+V_1**2/(2*g)-V_2**2/(2*g))*rho_water*g #Pa
p_2_= p_2/1000 #kPa
V_3= Q/(pi*D_3**2/4) #m/s
p_3= (p_2/(rho_water*g)+V_2**2/(2*g)-V_3**2/(2*g))*rho_water*g #Pa
p_3_= p_3/1000 #kPa



Question 7 (b)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The EGL (Energy Grade Line) is the total energy at each point along the flow. This includes the kinetic energy and the potential energy. Considering there is no friction loss in this exercise, the EGL should be a straight line.EGL= \frac{p}{\gamma}+\frac{V^2}{2g} +z

The HGL (Hydraulic Grade Line) represents the sum of the pressure head and the elevation head.This line will drop with each reduction in diameter due to the increase in velocity.HGL= \frac{p}{\gamma}+z

The difference between the lines is equal to the velocity head.

Solution

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/14.7S.png")
img

records.content:
Using the measurements from a Venturi Meter, you have calculated that the discharge through a pipe system should be 24 litres per second. To check this theoretical discharge, you have captured a volume of 4.0 m3 from the pipe system. It  took 3 minutes to collect this volume. Calculate the discharge coefficient.

Answer


0.926

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

To calculate the discharge coefficient (𝐶𝑑), we need to compare the actual discharge with the theoretical discharge obtained from the Venturi meter.
Don’t forget that you can calculate the discharge by dividing the total volume collected by the time taken.

Click here for a video explaining the problem

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->C_d= \frac{Q_a}{Q_t}

The actual discharge flow can be calcualted asQ= \frac{Vol}{t}

%%render long 3
Q_t= 24 #L/s
V= 4 #m3
t= 3#min
Q_a= (V*1000)/(t*60) #L/s
C_d= Q_a/Q_t

records.content:
Using the measurements from an Orifice Meter, you have calculated that the discharge through a pipe system should be 3.0 litres per second. You use a calibration tank with a surface area of 0.4 m2 to check this discharge. If the water level in the calibration tank rises at a rate of 4 mm/s, calculate the discharge coefficient.

Answer


0.533

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

To calculate the discharge coefficient (𝐶𝑑), we need to compare the actual discharge with the theoretical discharge obtained from the Venturi meter.
In this case, the actual discharge can be determined by multiplying the rate of water level rise by the surface area of the tank.

Click here for a video explaining the problem

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->C_d= \frac{Q_a}{Q_t}

The actual discharge flow can be calcualted asQ_a= AV

%%render long 3
Q_t= 3/1000 #m3/s
A= 0.4 #m2
V= 4/1000 #m/s
Q_a= A*V #m3/s
C_d= Q_a/Q_t

records.content:
Using the measurements from a Venturi Meter, you have calculated that the discharge through a pipe system should be 13 litres per second. Downstream of the Venturi Meter, you have installed a piezometer and a pitot tube. If the difference in water levels between the piezometer and the pitot tube is 70 mm, and the pipe diameter is 0.1 m, calculate the discharge coefficient.

Answer


0.708

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The water level in the piezometer is the HGL and the water level in the pitot tube is the EGL. The difference between those two water levels (EGL and HGL) is the velocity head.
From the velocity head, you can calculate the velocity and hence discharge in the pipe system.

Click here for a video explaining the problem

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->

Calculate the Velocity from the Pitot Tube Measurement:V= \sqrt {2g\Delta h}

An then the actual discharge can be calculated:Q_a= VA

Pay attention to units — use appropriate units for h and 𝑄 based on the formula applying.

%%render long 3
Q_t= 13 #L/s
h= 70 #mm
D= 0.1#m
V= sqrt(2*g*(h/1000)) #m/s
Q_a= (V)*pi*D**2/4 #m3/s
Q_a_m= Q_a*1000 #L/s
C_d= Q_a_m/Q_t

Problems for Lecture 16: Flow Measurement

records.content:
Water flows in an open channel of 2.0 m width. At some point in the channel, the water flows over a smooth submerged object that is 1.0 m high. If the water depth upstream of the object is 2.5 m and the depth downstream of the object is 0.5 m, calculate the discharge in the channel.

Answer


6.4 m^3/s

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Use a combination of the Bernoulli equation and the Continuity equation, in a similar approach to that used for a Venturi Meter. Recall that the cross-sectional area is given by the water depth multiplied by the channel width, and the HGL is always at the water surface. See the worked example at the start of Lecture 16 for detailed working.

Click here for a video explaining the problem

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->

Conservation of energy:E_1=E_2y_1+ \frac{V_1^2}{2g}=y_2+ \frac{V_2^2}{2g}

Conservation of mass:Q_1=Q_2V_1 \cdot A_1=V_2 \cdot A_2V_1 \cdot b \cdot y_1=V_2 \cdot b \cdot y_2V= \frac {Q}{A}

Reeplace the velocity equation in the Bernoulli eqution:y_1+ \frac{\frac {Q^2}{A_1^2}}{2g}=y_2+ \frac{\frac {Q^2}{A_2^2}}{2g}

The area is:A= b \cdot y

Substituting the area and rearranging the equationQ= b \cdot \sqrt {2 \cdot g \cdot (y_1 -y_2)} \cdot (\frac {y_1 \cdot y_2}{\sqrt {y_1 ^2 - y_2^2}})

%%render long

b= 2 #m
z= 1#m
y_1= 2.5#m
y_2= 0.5 #m

A_1= b*y_1 #m2
A_2= b*y_2#m2

Q_t= b*sqrt(2*g*(y_1-y_2))*((y_1*y_2)/(sqrt(y_1**2-y_2**2)))#m3/s

hierarchy.lvl2: Problems for Lecture 16: Flow Measurement
Problems for Lecture 16: Flow Measurement

records.content:
Water flows in an open channel of 1.2 m width. At some point in the channel, the water flows over a smooth submerged object. If the water depth upstream of the object is 1.2 m and the depth downstream of the object is 0.4 m, and the actual discharge in the channel is 1.6 m3/s, calculate the discharge coefficient

Answer


0.79

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Use a combination of the Bernoulli equation and the Continuity equation, in a similar approach to that used for a Venturi Meter. Recall that the cross-sectional area is given by the water depth multiplied by the channel width, and the HGL is always at the water surface. See the worked example at the start of Lecture 16 for detailed working.

The discharge coefficient is given as the ratio of the actual discharge to the theoretical discharge.

Click here for a video explaining the problem

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->

Conservation of energy:E_1=E_2y_1+ \frac{V_1^2}{2g}=y_2+ \frac{V_2^2}{2g}

Conservation of mass:Q_1=Q_2V_1 \cdot A_1=V_2 \cdot A_2V_1 \cdot b \cdot y_1=V_2 \cdot b \cdot y_2V= \frac {Q}{A}

Reeplace the velocity equation in the Bernoulli eqution:y_1+ \frac{\frac {Q^2}{A_1^2}}{2g}=y_2+ \frac{\frac {Q^2}{A_2^2}}{2g}

The area is:A= b \cdot y

Substituting the area and rearranging the equationQ= b \cdot \sqrt {2 \cdot g \cdot (y_1 -y_2)} \cdot (\frac {y_1 \cdot y_2}{\sqrt {y_1 ^2 - y_2^2}})

%%render long 3
b= 1.2 #m
y_1= 1.2#m
y_2= 0.4 #m
Q_a= 1.6 #m3/s
Q_t= b*sqrt(2*g*(y_1-y_2))*((y_1*y_2)/(sqrt(y_1**2-y_2**2)))#m3/s
C_d=Q_a/Q_t

hierarchy.lvl2: Problems for Lecture 16: Flow Measurement
Problems for Lecture 16: Flow Measurement

records.content:
A Venturi Meter has a pipe diameter of 120 mm and a throat diameter of 50 mm. When water is flowing through the Venturi Meter, the pressure in the pipe and the throat is recorded using piezometers. If the difference in water height in the two piezometers is 50 mm, calculate the discharge in the Venturi Meter.

Answer


1.975 L/s

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Use the equation for the theoretical discharge through a Venturi Meter, assuming no losses. If you want to solve this problem from first principles, use a combination of the Bernoulli equation and the Continuity equation and recognise that the water level in the piezometers will give the height of the HGL.

Click here for a video explaining the problem

Solution

%%render long 3
D_1= 120/1000 #m
D_2= 50/1000 #m
h_p=50/1000 #m
A_1= pi*(D_1)**2/4 #m2
A_2= pi*(D_2)**2/4#m2

Q= (sqrt(2*g*h_p)*((A_1*A_2)/(sqrt(A_1**2-A_2**2))))*1000 #L/s

hierarchy.lvl2: Problems for Lecture 16: Flow Measurement
Problems for Lecture 16: Flow Measurement

records.content:
A Venturi Meter has a pipe diameter of 120 mm and a throat diameter of 50 mm (i.e. the same as in Question 6). Now, however, the piezometers are replaced with a differential manometer filled with mercury (S.G. = 13.5). If the differential manometer registers a height difference of 20 mm, calculate the discharge passing
through the Venturi Meter.

Answer


0.004 m^3/s

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The difference between this and the last exercise is that the last one is with piezometers and this one with differential manometer. For that reason, you can not use \Delta h because that is the height of the mercury. The first step is to change \Delta h to \Delta p.

Use the equation for the theoretical discharge through a Venturi Meter, assuming no losses. If you want to solve this problem from first principles, use a combination of the Bernoulli equation and the Continuity equation. Note that in this case the pressure difference between the pipe and the throat is measured using a differential manometer, so you can’t directly use the \Delta h term as this is the difference in mercury levels. See Lecture 16 for an explanation of the approach when dealing with a differential manometer, and the second example problem in Lecture 17.

Click here for a video explaining the problem

Solution

%%render long 3
D_1= 120 #mm
D_2= 50 #mm 
SG= 13.5 
Delta_h= 20 #mm 
A_1= pi*(D_1/1000)**2/4 #m2
A_2= pi*(D_2/1000)**2/4#m2
rho_Hg= SG*rho_water #kg/m3
Delta_p= (rho_Hg-rho_water)*g*(Delta_h/1000) #Pa
Q= sqrt(2*(Delta_p/rho_water))*((A_1*A_2)/(sqrt(A_1**2-A_2**2))) #m3/s

hierarchy.lvl2: Problems for Lecture 16: Flow Measurement
Problems for Lecture 16: Flow Measurement

records.content:
A Venturi Meter has a pipe diameter of 180 mm and a throat diameter of 60
mm. When water is flowing through the Venturi Meter, the pressure in the pipe and
the throat is recorded using piezometers. If the difference in water height in the two
piezometers is 70 mm, and the discharge coefficient is 0.95, calculate the actual
discharge in the Venturi Meter.

Answer


3.17 L/s

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Calculate the theoretical discharge and use the discharge coefficient to calculate the actual discharge.

Use the equation for the theoretical discharge through a Venturi Meter, accounting for the actual discharge using the discharge coefficient. If you want to solve this problem from first principles, use a combination of the Bernoulli equation and the Continuity equation and recognise that the water level in the piezometers will give the height of the HGL.

Click here for a video explaining the problem

Solution

%%render long 2
D_1= 180/1000 #m
D_2= 60/1000 #m 
h= 70/1000 #m
C_d= 0.95
A_1= pi*(D_1)**2/4 #m2
A_2= pi*(D_2)**2/4#m2
Q_t= (sqrt(2*g*h)*((A_1*A_2)/(sqrt(A_1**2-A_2**2))))*1000 #L/s
Q_a= C_d*Q_t #L/s

records.content:
An orifice plate (internal diameter of 30 mm) is placed in a pipe with a diameter of 80 mm. With water flowing through the pipe, the pressure difference recorded by two piezometers attached to the orifice plate is 300 mm.

a. If the coefficient of discharge of the orifice plate is equal to 0.6, calculate the actual discharge flowing through the pipe.

b. With the aid of a sketch, show the main source of energy loss associated with water flow through an orifice plate. Include an illustration of the location of the two piezometers, and explain why we can use these measurements to determine the discharge.

Answer


1.04 L/s

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Click here for a video explaining the problem

Question 9 (a)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Calculate the theoretical discharge with the Equation for flow rate through an orifice. Then use the discharge coefficient to calculate the actual discharge.

Solution

%%render long 4
D_o= 30 #mm
D_p= 80 #mm 
Delta_h= 300 #mm 
C_d= 0.6
A_1= pi*(D_p/1000)**2/4 #m2
A_2= pi*(D_o/1000)**2/4#m2
Q_t= sqrt(2*g*Delta_h/1000)*((A_1*A_2)/(sqrt(A_1**2-A_2**2)))#m3/s
Q_a= C_d*Q_t *1000 #L/s



Question 9 (b)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The main source of energy loss when water flows through an orifice plate is due to the sudden contraction and expansion of the flow as it passes through the orifice. This energy loss is associated with flow separation at the edges of the orifice, leading to turbulent eddies and vortex formation.

You can use the same equation as for the theoretical discharge through a Venturi Meter, accounting for the actual discharge using the discharge coefficient. The internal diameter of the orifice plate is equivalent to the diameter of the throat.

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->

The main source of the energy loss is the flow separation from the sharp edge of the Orifice Plate. The piezometer locations are shown on the sketch below. Experimental  evidence shows that the pressure in the separation region is the same as the pressure at point 2 (the constriction in the orifice plate itself), which is why we can use these in a similar manner to those in the pipe and throat of a Venturi Meter.

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/15.9S.png")
img

records.content:
A Venturi Meter has a pipe diameter of 120 mm and a throat diameter of 50 mm. When water is flowing through the Venturi Meter, the pressure in the pipe and the throat is recorded using piezometers. The difference in water height in the two piezometers is 80 mm. Downstream of the Venturi Meter, water passes through a nozzle and exits as a free jet to the atmosphere.

a. If the jet velocity is 3 m/s and the jet diameter is 0.02 m, calculate the discharge coefficient.

b. Calculate the pressure within the pipe.

Answer

a) 0.37

b) 4496.5 Pa

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Click here for a video explaining the problem

Question 10 (a)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The discharge coefficient is the ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge. The former can be calculated using the conservation of mass, while the latter can be determined using the equation for the theoretical discharge through a Venturi Meter. If you want to solve this problem from first principles, use a combination of the Bernoulli equation and the Continuity equation to determine the theoretical discharge, and recognise that the water level in the piezometers will give the height of the HGL.

Solution

%%render long 5
D_1= 120/1000 #m
D_2= 50/1000 #m
Delta_h= 80/1000 #m 
V_jet= 3 #m/s
D_jet= 0.02 #m
A_1= pi*(D_1)**2/4 #m2
A_2= pi*(D_2)**2/4 #m2
Q_t= sqrt(2*g*Delta_h)*((A_1*A_2)/(sqrt(A_1**2-A_2**2)))#m3/s
A_jet= pi*(D_jet)**2/4#m2
Q_a= V_jet*A_jet #m3/s
C_d= Q_a/Q_t



Question 10 (b)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

We can use the Bernoulli equation (between a point in the pipe and a point in the jet) to calculate the pressure within the pipe. Use the actual flow conditions in this solution.

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->

From the Bernoulli equation we have:\frac{p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{V_1^2}{2 g}=\frac{p_3}{\rho g}+\frac{V_3^2}{2 g}

Assuming p_3=0 because the pressure is atmospheric, and calculating V_1 as V_1=Q_a/A_1, we can solve p_1.

%%render long 3
V_1= Q_a/(pi*D_1**2/4) #m/s
p_1= (V_jet**2/(2*g)-V_1**2/(2*g))*rho_water*g #Pa

records.content:
Water flows up pipe AB (5 m long, 40 mm diameter) and along BC (3 m long, 30 mm diameter) at 1.75 L/s. If the measured pressure at A is 250 kPa, and the pipe friction loss between A and C is 1.45 m, find the pressure at C. Neglect local energy losses caused by the diameter change and bend at B.

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/85.png")
img



Answer


\;184.63 \; kPa

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Apply the Energy equation between points A and C.
Note that points A and C are inside the pipe and aren’t free jets

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{flalign}
&z_A+\frac{p_A}{\rho g}+\frac{v_A^2}{2g}=z_C+\frac{p_C}{\rho g}+\frac{v_C^2}{2g} + h_f \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&z_A = 0 \text{ (datum)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&\frac{p_A}{\rho g}+\frac{v_A^2}{2g}=z_C+\frac{p_C}{\rho g}+\frac{v_C^2}{2g} + h_f \\
& p_C = (\frac{p_A}{\rho g}+\frac{v_A^2}{2g}-z_C-\frac{v_C^2}{2g} - h_f){\rho g} \\
\end{flalign}

%%render long 3
D_AB=40/1000 #m 
L_AB=5#m 
D_BC=30/1000 #m
L_BC=3#m 
Q=1.75/1000 #m3/s
p_A=250*1000#Pa
h_f= 1.45 #m 

A_AB= pi*D_AB**2/4 #m2
V_A= Q/A_AB #m/s
A_BC= pi*D_BC**2/4 #m2
V_C= Q/A_BC #m/s

p_C= (p_A/(rho_water*g)+V_A**2/(2*g)-L_AB-V_C**2/(2*g)-h_f)*rho_water*g #Pa

A vertical pipe of 1.5 m diameter and 20 m length has a pressure head at the upper end of 6.3 m. When the flow of water is such that the mean velocity is 5.6 m/s, the pipe friction head loss hf= 1.09 m. Find the pressure head at the lower end of the pipe when the flow is a)downwards and b) upwards.

Answer

$
a) ;25.2 ; m ;;

b) ;27.4; m
$

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Apply the Energy equation.

Question 2 (a)

Solution

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/L18Q2s.png")
img



 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{flalign}
&z_1+\frac{p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=z_2+\frac{p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_f \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&v_1 = v_2 \text{ (constant area)} \\
&z_2 = 0 \text{ (datum)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&z_1+\frac{p_1}{\rho g}=\frac{p_2}{\rho g} + h_f \\
&h_2 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} = z_1+\frac{p_1}{\rho g}- h_f \\
\end{flalign}

%%render long 3
h_1 = 6.3 #m
z_1 = 20 #m
h_f = 1.09 #m
h_2 = +z_1+h_1-h_f #m



Question 2 (b)

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{flalign}
&z_1+\frac{p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g} + h_f=z_2+\frac{p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&v_1 = v_2 \text{ (constant area)} \\
&z_2 = 0 \text{ (datum)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&z_1+\frac{p_1}{\rho g}+ h_f=\frac{p_2}{\rho g}  \\
&h_2 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} = z_1+\frac{p_1}{\rho g}+ h_f \\
\end{flalign}

%%render long 3
h_2 = +z_1+h_1+h_f #m

records.content:
If h = 10.5 m in the figure below and the pressures at A and B are 170 and 275 kPa respectively, find the direction of flow and the pipe friction headloss. Assume the liquid has a specific gravity of 0.85.

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/18_energy_4.png")
img



Answer


\;2.09 \;m\; ; \;flow \; is \; from\; B \; to \; A

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{flalign}
&\text{Assume the flow is from A to B: } \\
&z_A+\frac{p_A}{\rho g}+\frac{v_A^2}{2g}=z_B+\frac{p_B}{\rho g}+\frac{v_B^2}{2g} + h_f     \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&z_B = 0 \text{ (datum)} \\
&v_1=v_2 = 0 \text{ (identical diameters)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&\frac{p_A}{\rho g}=\frac{p_B}{\rho g}+ h_f \\
&h_f = \frac{p_A}{\rho g}-\frac{p_B}{\rho g} \\
\end{flalign}

%%render long 2
p_A = 170*1000 #Pa
p_B = 275* 1000 #Pa
S = 0.85
rho = S * rho_water
Z_A = 10.5 #m

h_f = p_A/(rho*g)+ Z_A -p_B/(rho*g) #m



 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->

A negative headloss means the flow direction  assumption was wrong. The flow occurs from B to A.

records.content:
A 50 mm diameter siphon is drawing oil (s = 0.82) from an oil reservoir as shown below. If the headloss between points 1 and 2 is 1.50 m and between points 2 and 3 is 2.40 m, find the discharge of oil from the siphon and the oil pressure at point 2.

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/18_energy_5.png")
img



Answer


\;9.1 \;L/s \; ; \;-37 \;kPa

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Apply the Energy equation between Points 1 and 3.
Then apply the Energy equation between Points  1 and 2.

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{flalign}
&\text{Apply the Energy equation between Points 1 and 3.} \\
&z_1+\frac{p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=z_3+\frac{p_3}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_{f1-2}+h_{f2-3}    \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&p_1 = 0 \text{ (atmospheric pressure)} \\
&v_1 = 0 \text{ (large tank area)} \\
&z_1 = 0 \text{ (datum)} \\
&p_2 = 0 \text{ (free jet)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&0=z_2+\frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_{f1-2}+h_{f2-3} \\
&v_2=\sqrt{(-z_2 -h_{f1-2}-h_{f2-3})2g} \\
\end{flalign}\begin{flalign}
\\
\\
&\text{Apply the Energy equation between Points 1 and 2.} \\
&z_1+\frac{p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=z_2+\frac{p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_{f1-2} \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&p_1 = 0 \text{ (atmospheric pressure)} \\
&v_1 = 0 \text{ (large tank area)} \\
&z_1 = 0 \text{ (datum)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&0=z_2+\frac{p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_{f1-2} \\
&p_2=(-z_2 - \frac{v_2^2}{2g} - h_{f1-2}){\rho g} \\
\end{flalign}

%%render long 4

z_3 = -5 #m
h_f_12 = 1.5 #m
h_f_23 = 2.4 #m

v_3 = sqrt((-z_3 -h_f_12-h_f_23)*2*g) #m/s

D =50/1000 #m
A = pi*D**2/4 #m2
Q = V_3 *A #m3/s
Q_Lps=Q*1000 #L/s


z_2 = 2 #m
V_2 = V_3 #m/s
S_oil = 0.82
rho_oil = S_oil*rho_water
p_2 =  (-z_2 - V_2**2/(2*g) -h_f_12)*rho_oil*g #Pa

records.content:
The friction losses in the suction pipe of the system shown in the figure below aregiven by the equation hf= 1.6 \frac{v^2}{2 g}. The barometer reading is 90 kPa and the suction pipe has a velocity of 1.8 m/s. What is the maximum allowable value of z if the liquid is
a) water at 20 ℃;
b) petrol with a vapour pressure of 49 kPa and a specific weight of 8 kN/m3?

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/18_energy_7.png")
img



Answer

$
a);8.51 ;m;

b);4.70 ; m;
$

Question 7(a)

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{flalign}
\text{Work in absolute pressure}& \\
&z_A+\frac{p_A}{\rho g}+\frac{v_A^2}{2g}=z_B+\frac{p_B}{\rho g}+\frac{v_B^2}{2g} + h_f     \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&z_A = 0 \text{ (datum)} \\
&p_A = p_{atm}  \text{ (atmospheric pressure)} \\
&v_A= 0 \text{ (large area)} \\
&p_B = p_{vap}  \text{ (absolute vapour pressure)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&\frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g}=z_B+\frac{p_{vap}}{\rho g}+\frac{v_B^2}{2g} + h_f \\
&z_B = \frac{p_{atm}-p_{vap}}{\rho g}-\frac{v_B^2}{2g} - h_f \\
\end{flalign}

%%render long 2
p_atm=90*1000 #Pa
p_vap=2.34*1000 #Pa abs
v_B=1.8 #m/s
rho=rho_water #kg/m3
h_f= 1.6*v_B**2/(2*g) #m

z_B = (p_atm-p_vap)/(rho*g)-v_B**2/(2*g)-h_f #m



Question 7(b)

Solution

%%render long 2
p_atm=90*1000 #Pa
p_vap=49*1000 #Pa abs
v_B=1.8 #m/s
gamma_gas=8000 #N/m3
rho=gamma_gas/g #kg/m3
h_f= 1.6*v_B**2/(2*g) #m

z_B = (p_atm-p_vap)/(rho*g)-v_B**2/(2*g)-h_f #m



%%render long 2
gamma = 8 #kN/m3
p_nu = 49 #kN/m2 abs

z_B = (90-p_nu)/gamma-0.429 #m

records.content:
Water flows between two tanks as shown in the figure below. The flowrate through the pipe is 16 l/s, and the saturation vapour pressure of the water is -93 kPa. Answer the following question:
a). What is the total headloss in the system of flow between the tanks ?
b). At what diameter d of the pipe constriction will cavitation start? Assume that the additional head loss due to the constriction is negligible.

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/18_energy_8.png")
img



Answer

$
a);15 ;m;

b); 31.3 ; mm;
$

Question 8 (a)

Solution

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/18_energy_8s.png")
img



 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{flalign}
&z_1+\frac{p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=z_2+\frac{p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_{f} \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&p_1 = 0 \text{ (atmospheric pressure)} \\
&v_1 = 0 \text{ (large tank area)} \\
&p_2 = 0 \text{ (atmospheric pressure)} \\
&v_2 = 0 \text{ (large tank area)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&z_1=z_2+h_{f} \\
&h_{f}=z_1-z_2 = 20 - 5 = 15 m
\end{flalign}

Question 8 (b)

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{flalign}
&z_1+\frac{p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=z_3+\frac{p_3}{\rho g}+\frac{v_3^2}{2g} + h_{f13} \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&p_1 = 0 \text{ (atmospheric pressure)} \\
&v_1 = 0 \text{ (large tank area)} \\
&z_3 = 0 \text{ (datum)} \\
&p_3 = p_{vap} \text{ (cavitation will start at vapour pressure)} \\
&v_3 = \frac{4 Q}{\pi D^2} \\
\text{Assume, }& \\
&\text{Headloss to Point 3 is half the total headloss.} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&z_1=\frac{p_{vap}}{\rho g}+\frac{8 Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^4} + h_{f13} \\
&D= (\frac{8  Q^2}{(z_1 -\frac{p_{vap}}{\rho g}-h_{f13})\pi^2 g})^{0.25}   \\
\end{flalign}

%%render long 3
z_1 = 20 #m
p_vap = -93 *1000 #Pa
h_f13 = 15 #m
Q=16/1000 #m3/s

D_3 = ((8*Q**2)/((pi**2)*g*(z_1-(p_3/(rho_water*g))-(1/2)*h_f13)))**(1/4)#m

A 150 mm diameter pipe is discharging water through a turbine. The velocity in the pipe just upstream of the turbine is 36 m/s and the pressure 100 m. The pipe diameter remains the same on the downstream side of the turbine. Calculate the maximum power that the turbine
can deliver if losses are ignored.

records.content:
A pump with efficiency 90% lift water between two reservoir with a water level difference of 155 m at a flow rate of 7.5 m3 /s. The friction head loss in the pipe is 13 m. What is the input power requirement of the pump?

Answer


\;13.73 \;MW\;

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{flalign}
&z_1+\frac{p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}+h_{pump}=z_2+\frac{p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_{f} \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&p_1 = 0 \text{ (atmospheric pressure)} \\
&v_1 = 0 \text{ (large tank area)} \\
&z_1 = 0 \text{ (Datum} \\
&p_2 = 0 \text{ (atmospheric pressure)} \\
&v_2 = 0 \text{ (large tank area)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&h_{pump}=z_2+ + h_{f}
\end{flalign}

%%render long 2
h_f = 13 #m
z_2 = 155 #m
Q = 7.5 #m3/s 
h_pump = z_2+h_f #m
P_out = (rho_water*g*h_pump*Q)/1000000 #MW

eta = 0.9
P_in = P_out/eta #MW

records.content:
After entering a pump through a 180 mm diameter pipe at 35 kPa, oil (s = 0.82) leaves the pump through a 120 mm diameter pipe at 120 kPa.
The suction and discharge sides of the pump are at the same elevation. Find the rate at which energy is delivered to the oil by the pump if the flow rate is 70 L/s.

Answer


\;6.83 \;kW\;

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\frac{p_1}{\gamma}+\frac{V_1^2}{2g}+z_1 + h_p=\frac{p_2}{\gamma}+\frac{V_2^2}{2g}+z_2 + h_f\\

z_1 = z_2 and h_f = 0h_p = \frac{p_2}{\gamma}+\frac{V_2^2}{2g}- \frac{p_1}{\gamma}-\frac{V_1^2}{2g}

%%render long 3
Q = 70/1000 #m3/s
D_1 = 180/1000 #m

p_2 = 120*1000 #Pa

s = 0.82
h_p = p_2/(s*rho_water*g)+ V_2**2/(2*g)-p_1/(s*rho_water*g)-V_1**2/(2*g) #m

records.content:
Water is supplied from a reservoir to a powerhouse that is located at an elevation of 325 m below that of the reservoir surface (see figure). The jet has a nozzle diameter of 250 mm with a velocity of 75 m/s through the nozzle.Determine the power loss due to friction between the
reservoir and the jet, as well as the potential power of the jet. Draw the energy and hydraulic grade lines of the system assuming that 15 % of the friction losses occur in the nozzle.

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/18_energy_12.png")
img



Answer


\;1383 \;kW\;10.35\;MW\;

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The maximum power will be obtained if the turbine fully converts the velocity head into a pressure head.

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{flalign}
&z_1+\frac{p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=z_2+\frac{p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g} +h_{f}    \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&p_1 = 0 \text{ (atmospheric pressure)} \\
&v_1 = 0 \text{ (large tank area)} \\
&z_2 = 0 \text{ (datum)} \\
&p_2 = 0 \text{ (free jet)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&z_1=\frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_{f} \\
&h_{f}=z_1-\frac{v_2^2}{2g} \\
\end{flalign}

%%render long 3
z_1 = 325 #m
v_2 = 75 #m/s

h_f = z_1 -v_2**2/(2*g) #m
D_2=250/1000 #m
A_2= pi*D_2**2/4 #m2
Q = A_2*v_2 #m3/s
P_lost = (rho_water*g*Q*h_f)/1e6 #MW

h_j = v_2**2/(2*g) #m
P_jet = (rho_water*g*Q*h_j)/1e6 #MW

records.content:
A rectangular conduit has side lengths of 2 and 4 m. Calculate the diameter of the circular pipe that will have the same hydraulic radius (and thus the same headloss) as this conduit.

Answer

2.67 m

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The hydraulic radius is defined as the cross-sectional area divided by the wetted perimeter (i.e. the circumference of the section).

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{flalign}
&R_h = \frac{A}{P}\\ \\
&\text{For a rectangular conduit with side lengths a and b} \\
&R_{h,rect} = \frac{a b}{2(a+b)}\\ \\
&\text{For a circular conduit with diameter D} \\
&R_{h,circ} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4 \pi D} = \frac{D}{4}\\ \\
&\text{For the same hydrauilc radius} \\
&R_{h,circ} = R_{h,rect} \\
&D = \frac{2a b}{(a+b)}\\ 
\end{flalign}

%%render long 2
a= 2#m
b= 4#m
D= (2*a*b)/(a+b) #m

records.content:
A circular pipe with a diameter of 100 mm, a length of 1 500 m and an absolute roughness of 0.5 mm carries water at a temperature of 15°C and a velocity of 1 m/s.a. Calculate the headloss in the pipe.b. Calculate the headloss if the circular pipe is replaced with a square pipe that has the same cross-sectional area.

Answer

a) 24.0 m

b) b = 0.089 m; Re = 77739; f = 0.033; hf = 28.1 m

Question 4 (a)

Solution

%%render long 4
D=100/1000 #m
L=150 #m
e=0.5/1000 #m
nu = 1.14e-6 #m2/s
v= 1 #m/s
Re = (v*D)/nu #Turbulent
f= (1/(-1.8*log10((e/(3.7*D))**1.11+(6.9/Re))))**2
h_f= (f*L*v**2)/(2*g*D) #m



Question 4 (b)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint\begin{flalign}
&\text{Find an expression for the hydraulic radius of a circular section.} \\ 
&\text{Write the diameter as a function of the hydraulic radius.} \\ 
&\text{Replace the D in the Reynolds, Haaland and Darcy-Weisbach equations with the hydraulic radius.} \\ 
&\text{Solve the problem for a square section using the hydraulic radius formulation.}
\end{flalign}

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{flalign}
&\text{For a square conduit with side length a} \\
&A_{rect} = A_{circ} \\
&a^2 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \\
&a = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}D}{2} \\ \\
&\text{The pipe friction loss equations were developed for a circular section. Thus, use a circular section to find the link between hydraulic radius and Diameter.} \\ \\
&\text{For a circular conduit with diameter D} \\
&R_{h} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4 \pi D} = \frac{D}{4}\\
&D = 4R_{h} \\ \\
&\text{Amend the Reynolds, Haaland and Darcy-Weisbach by replacing D with the relationship above. Then solve the problem for a square section using the hydrauilc radius formulations.} \\
&R_h = \frac{A}{P}\\
&R_{h,square} = \frac{a^2}{4a} = \frac{a}{4}\\
\end{flalign}

%%render long 
a= (sqrt(pi)*D)/2 #m
R_h = a/4 #m
Re = (v*4*R_h)/nu #Turbulent
f= (1/(-1.8*log10((e/(3.7*4*R_h))**1.11+(6.9/Re))))**2
h_f= (f*L*v**2)/(2*g*4*R_h) #m

records.content:
A triangular conduit has three equal sides, each with a length of 4 m. Calculate the head loss in 100 m of the conduit flowing full at a velocity of 2 m/s. The absolute roughness of the conduit is estimated as 0.2 mm.

Answer

0.107 m

 ```{Dropdown} Hint\begin{flalign}
&\text{Find an expression for the hydraulic radius of a circular section.} \\ 
&\text{Write the diameter as a function of the hydraulic radius.} \\ 
&\text{Replace the D in the Reynolds, Haaland and Darcy-Weisbach equations with the hydraulic radius.} \\ 
&\text{Solve the problem for a square section using the hydraulic radius formulation.}
\end{flalign}

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{flalign}
&\text{The pipe friction loss equations were developed for a circular section. Thus, use a circular section to find the link between hydraulic radius and pipe diameter.} \\ \\
&\text{For a circular conduit with diameter D} \\
&R_{h} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4 \pi D} = \frac{D}{4}\\
&D = 4R_{h} \\ \\
&\text{Amend the Reynolds, Haaland and Darcy-Weisbach by replacing D with the relationship above.} \\
&\text{Then solve the problem for a triangular section using the hydraulic radius formulations.} \\
&R_h=\frac{A}{P}=\frac{a^2 \sqrt{3}}{4} \frac{1}{3a} = \frac{a \sqrt{3}}{12} 
\end{flalign}

%%render long 6
a= 4 #m
L=100 #m
v=2 #m/s
e = 0.2/1000 #mm
R_h = (a*sqrt(3))/12 #m
Re = (v*4*R_h)/nu #Turbulent
f= (1/(-1.8*log10((e/(3.7*4*R_h))**1.11+(6.9/Re))))**2
h_f= (f*L*v**2)/(2*g*4*R_h) #m

records.content:
Consider a 100 mm internal diameter plastic pipe with a length of 1000 m and an absolute roughness of 0.01 mm.

a.	What should the secondary loss coefficient k be to have a local loss one tenth of the friction loss at Re = 4000?

b.	Calculate the friction, secondary and total head losses for the pipe at a Re = 105.  Use the local loss coefficient found in (a).

c.	Did the proportional contribution of the local loss to the total head loss increase or decrease between (a) and (b)? Why did it change?

Answer

a) 40.5

b) 14.92

Question 7 (a)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

With the Reynolds number, we can calculate the velocity, and from that, the friction loss. Then, calculate the secondary loss coefficient, knowing that it is 1/10 of the friction loss.

Solution

%%render long 3
D= 100/1000 #m 
L= 1000#m 
e= 0.01/1000 #m 
Re= 4000 
#Re_b= 10**5
nu= 1.14*10**-6 #m2/s
v= nu*Re/D
A= pi*D**2/4 #m2
Q= v*A #m3/s

f= (1/(-1.8*log10((e/(3.7*D))**1.11+(6.9/Re))))**2
h_f_a= 8*f*L*Q**2/(g*pi**2*D**5)#m
h_s_a= 0.1*h_f_a #m
k_= h_s_a*g*2/(v**2)



Question 7 (b)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Calculate the new velocity with Reynolds.

Solution

%%render long 3
Re= 10**5
v= nu*Re/D
A= pi*D**2/4 #m2
Q= v*A #m3/s

f= (1/(-1.8*log10((e/(3.7*D))**1.11+(6.9/Re))))**2
h_f_b= 8*f*L*Q**2/(g*pi**2*D**5)#m
h_s_b = K_*v**2/(2*g) #m
h_T_b= h_s_b+h_f_b #m



Question 7 (c)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The contribution of the secondary loss to the total head can be calculated as:contribution_{h_s}=\frac{h_s}{h_T}*100

Solution

%%render long 3
h_T_a= h_f_a+h_s_a #m
contribution_a= (h_s_a/h_T_a)*100 #percent
contribution_b= (h_s_b/h_T_b)*100 #percent



 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->

k is constant, but f reduces when Re changes from 4000 to 100000.

records.content:
Calculate the equivalent length of two pipes in series. Pipe 1 has a length of 1000 m, a diameter of 300 mm and a friction factor f of 0.024. Pipe 2 has a length of 500 m, a diameter of 100 mm and a friction factor f of 0.024. For the equivalent pipe, use a diameter of 300 mm and a friction factor f of 0.024. Work from basic principles.

Answer


\;122500 \; m

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

For pipes in series, the equivalent pipe can be developed using:

Conservation of Mass Q_E= Q_1=Q_2

Conservation of Energy h_{fE}= h_f1+h_f2

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->h_f= \frac {fLV^2}{2gD}\\h_f= \frac {8fLQ^2}{\pi ^2gD^5}

Using the energy losses equation and the conservation of energy we have the following:(\frac{8fLQ^2}{\pi^2 gD^5})_E=(\frac{8fLQ^2}{\pi^2 gD^5})_1 +(\frac{8fLQ^2}{\pi^2 gD^5})_2\\\frac{f_EL_E}{D_E^5}=\frac{f_1L_1}{D_1^5}+\frac{f_2L_2}{D_2^5}\\\frac{L_E}{D_E^5}=\frac{L_1}{D_1^5}+\frac{L_2}{D_2^5}\\

%%render long 
L_1= 1000 #m 
D_1= 300/1000 #m 
f_1= 0.024 
L_2= 500 #m 
D_2= 100/1000 #m 
f_2= 0.024 
D_E= 300/1000 #m 
f_E= 0.024
L_E= (L_1/(D_1)**5+L_2/D_2**5)*D_E**5 #m

records.content:
A pipe with an internal diameter of 500 mm, a roughness coefficient f = 0.020 and a length of 1 000 m is placed in series with a pipe with an internal diameter of 1500 mm, a roughness coefficient f = 0.022 and a length of 1 000 m. The two pipes are used to link two reservoirs with a head differential of 30 m. Ignore local losses and calculate the following:

a. The length of the 1 500 mm diameter pipe above that will be equivalent to the two pipes in series.

b. The flow rate between the reservoirs.

c. The energy head where the two pipes are joined.

Answer


a) \;221 909 \; m


b) \;751.5 \; L/s

Question 2 (a)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

For pipes in series, the equivalent pipe can be developed using:

Conservation of Mass Q_E= Q_1=Q_2

Conservation of Energy h_{fE}= h_f1+h_f2

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->(\frac{8fLQ^2}{\pi^2 gD^5})_E=(\frac{8fLQ^2}{\pi^2 gD^5})_1 +(\frac{8fLQ^2}{\pi^2 gD^5})_2\frac{f_EL_E}{D_E^5}=\frac{f_1L_1}{D_1^5}+\frac{f_2L_2}{D_2^5}

%%render long 3
L_1= 1000  #m 
D_1= 500/1000 #m 
f_1= 0.02 
L_2= 1000  #m 
D_2= 1500/1000 #m 
f_2= 0.022
D_E= 1500/1000 #m 
f_E= 0.022
h_f= 30 #m 
L_E= ((f_1*L_1/(D_1)**5+f_2*L_2/D_2**5)*D_E**5)/f_E #m



Question 2 (b)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The flow rate can be solved by applying the Darcy-Weisbach equation to the equivalent pipe.

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->h_f =\frac{8f_EL_EQ_E^2}{\pi^2 gD_E^5}\\
Q^2=\frac{h_f \pi^2 gD_E^5}{8 f_E L_E}

Question 2 (c)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Apply the Energy Equation between point A and B.

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->

Conservation of energy\begin{align}
&z_A+\frac{p_A}{\rho g}+\frac{v_A^2}{2g}=H_B + h_f \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
&h_f =\frac{8f_EL_EQ_E^2}{\pi^2 gD_E^5}\\
&z_A+ \frac {P_A}{\rho g}+\frac {V_A^2}{2g}=H_B+\frac{8f_EL_EQ_E^2}{\pi^2 gD_E^5}\\
\text{But,   }& \\
&v_A = 0 \text{ (large area)} \\
&p_A = 0 \text{ (atmospheric)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&30= H_B+\frac{8f_EL_EQ_E^2}{\pi^2 gD_E^5}\\
\end{align}

%%render long 
H_B= h_f-(8*f_1*L_1*Q_E**2)/(pi**2*g*D_1**5) #m

A pipeline 300 m long discharges freely at a point 50 m lower than the watersurface at the intake (see figure below). The first 200 m of pipe has a diameter of 350 mm and the remaining 100 m has a diameter of 250 mm. The junction point C is 40 m below the intake water surface level. Ignore secondary losses and work from basic principles to answer the following questions:

a) Find the rate of discharge, assuming f = 0.06 for both pipes.

b) Find the pressure head just upstream of C.

c) Find the pressure head just downstream of C.

img = Image.open(r"figures/123.png")
img



Answer


a) \;264 \; L/s


b) \;26.46\; m


c) \;25.37 \; m

Question 3 (a)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Apply the Energy equation between reservoir and pipe outlet.

For pipes in series, the equivalent pipe can be developed using:

Conservation of Mass Q_E= Q_1=Q_2

Conservation of Energy h_{fE}= h_f1+h_f2

Solution

img = Image.open(r"figures/123Sa.png")
img



 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->h_f= \frac {fLV^2}{2gD}\\
h_f= \frac {8fLQ^2}{\pi ^2gD^5}

Using the energy losses equation and the conservation of energy we have the following:\frac{8f_EL_EQ^2}{\pi^2 gD_E^5}=\frac{8f_1L_1Q^2}{\pi^2 gD_1^5}+\frac{8f_2L_2Q^2}{\pi^2 gD_2^5}\\\frac{f_EL_E}{D_E^5}=\frac{f_1L_1}{D_1^5}+\frac{f_2L_2}{D_2^5}\\

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->

After calculating the length, the Bernoulli equation between point 1 and 2 can be used:\begin{align}
&z_1+\frac{p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=z_2+\frac{p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g}+ h_f \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&v_1 = 0 \text{ (large area)} \\
&p_1 = p_2 = 0 \text{ (atmospheric)} \\
&z_1 = 50 \text{ (given)} \\
&z_2 = 0 \text{ (datum)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&z_1=\frac{V_2^2}{2 g}+h_{fE}\\
&h_{fE}=z_1-\frac{V_2^2}{2 g}\\
\end{align}h_f= \frac {fLV^2}{2gD}\\
h_f= \frac {8fLQ^2}{\pi ^2gD^5}

We can use the following equation:\frac{8f_EL_EQ^2}{\pi^2gD_E^5}=z_1-\frac{8Q^2}{\pi^2gD_2^4}\\Q^2(\frac{8f_EL_E}{\pi^2gD_E^5}+\frac{8}{\pi^2gD_2^4})=z_1\\Q=\sqrt{\frac{z_1}{\frac{8f_EL_E}{\pi^2gD_E^5}+\frac{8}{\pi^2gD_2^4}}}

%%render long
L_E=300 #m
z_2=50 #m
L_1= 200 #m
D_1= 350/1000 #m
L_2= 100 #m
D_2= 250/1000 #m
z_1=40 #m
f=0.06
D_E=0.25 #m
L_E= (((f*L_1)/D_1**5+(f*L_2)/D_2**5)*D_E**5)/f #m



%%render long
Z_1=50 #m
Q= sqrt(Z_1/((8*f*L_E)/(pi**2*g*D_E**5)+(8)/(pi**2*g*D_2**4))) #m3/s
Q_= Q*1000 #L/s



Question 3 (b)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Apply the Energy equation between reservoir and pipe outlet.

Note that Point 2 is in the large diameter pipe.

img = Image.open(r"figures/123Sb.png")
img



Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{align}
&z_1+\frac{p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=z_2+\frac{p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g}+ h_f \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&v_1 = 0 \text{ (large area)} \\
&p_1 = 0 \text{ (atmospheric)} \\
&z_1 = 40 \text{ (given)} \\
&z_2 = 0 \text{ (datum)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&\frac{p_2}{\rho g}=z_1-\frac{V_2^2}{2 g}-h_{f}
\end{align}

%%render long 2
Z_1=40 #m
D=350/1000 #m
L=200 #m
V_2= Q/(pi*D**2/4) #m/s
h_f= (8*f*L*Q**2)/(pi**2*g*D**5)
h_pressure= Z_1-V_2**2/(2*g)-h_f #m



Question 3 (c)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Apply the Energy equation between reservoir and pipe outlet.

Note that Point c is in the smaller diameter pipe.

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{align}
&z_c+\frac{p_c}{\rho g}+\frac{v_c^2}{2g}=z_{out}+\frac{p_{out}}{\rho g}+\frac{v_{out}^2}{2g}+ h_f \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&v_{out} = 0 \text{ (large area)} \\
&p_{out} = 0 \text{ (atmospheric)} \\
&z_c = 40 \text{ (given)} \\
&z_{out} = 0 \text{ (datum)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&\frac{p_c}{\rho g}=Z_c-\frac{V_c^2}{2 g}-h_{f}
\end{align}

%%render long 2
Z_C=40 #m
D=250/1000 #m
V_C= Q/(pi*D**2/4) #m/s
#h_f= (8*f*L*Q**2)/(pi**2*g*D_2**5)
h_pressure= Z_C-V_C**2/(2*g)-h_f #m

records.content:
Two reservoirs are connected by two parallel pipes with diameters of 100 mm and 200 mm respectively. Both pipes have lengths of 1000 m and friction coefficients f = 0.035. Work from basic principles to estimate the equivalent diameter of the two pipes. Assume that the equivalent pipe has a length of 1000 m and an f of 0.035.

Answer


\;213 \; mm

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

For pipes in series, the parallel pipe can be developed using:Q_E=Q_1+Q_2

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->Q_E=Q_1+Q_2

Calculating Q with Darcy-Weisbach equation:h_f=\frac{fLV^2}{2gD}\\h_f=\frac{8fLQ^2}{\pi^2gD^5}\\Q=\sqrt{\frac{\pi^2gD^5h_f}{8fL}}\\Q_E=Q_1+Q_2\\\sqrt{\frac{\pi^2gD_E^5h_{fE}}{8f_EL_E}}=\sqrt{\frac{\pi^2gD_1^5h_{f1}}{8f_1L_1}}+\sqrt{\frac{\pi^2gD_2^5h_{f2}}{8f_2L_2}}

As in parallel pipes:h_{fE}=h_{f1}=h_{f2}\\\\\sqrt{\frac{D_E^5}{f_EL_E}}=\sqrt{\frac{D_1^5}{f_1L_1}}+\sqrt{\frac{D_2^5}{f_2L_2}}\\D_E=((\sqrt{\frac{D_1^5}{f_1L_1}}+\sqrt{\frac{D_2^5}{f_2L_2}})^2(f_EL_E))^{1/5}

%%render long
D_1=100/1000 #m
D_2=200/1000 #m
L=1000 #m
f=0.035
D_E= ((sqrt(D_1**5/(f*L))+sqrt(D_2**5/(f*L)))**2*(f*L))**(1/5) #m
D_E_= D_E*1000 #mm

A 200 mm diameter pipe with length of 500 m and roughness coefficient f = 0.025 is placed in parallel with a 500 mm diameter pipe with length 500 m and f = 0.030. The total flowrate in the system is 600 l/s. Ignore minor losses.

a. Calculate the diameter of an equivalent pipe with length 500 m and f = 0.030. Work from basic principles.

b. Use the equivalent pipe to calculate the headloss in the system.

c. Calculate the flow rate in each of the two parallel pipes.

d. Check that the sum of the flows in the two pipes equals the known system flow (or is close to it).

Question 5 (a)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

For pipes in parallel, the equivalent pipe can be developed using:

Conservation of Mass Q_E= Q_1+Q_2

Conservation of Energy h_E= hf_1=hf_2

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->

The equation for h_f is:h_f= \frac{8fLQ^2}{\pi^2 gD^5}\\

Using the equation of h_f to calculate Q:Q= \sqrt {\frac{\pi^2 h_fgD^5}{8fL}}\\

Replacing that equation in the conservation of mass(\sqrt {\frac{\pi^2 h_fgD^5}{8fL}})_E=(\sqrt {\frac{\pi^2 h_fgD^5}{8fL}})_1 +(\sqrt {\frac{\pi^2 h_fgD^5}{8fL}})_2\\(\sqrt {\frac{D^5}{fL}})_E=(\sqrt {\frac{D^5}{fL}})_1 +(\sqrt {\frac{D^5}{fL}})_2\\

%%render long 
D= 200/1000 #m 
L= 500 #m
f= 0.025 
D_p= 500/1000 #m 
L_p= 500 #m 
f_p=0.03
Q= 600/1000 #m3/s
L_E= 500 #m 
f_E= 0.03
D_E= (((D**5/(f*L))**0.5+(D_p**5/(f_p*L_p))**0.5)**2*f_E*L_E)**(1/5) #m



Question 5 (b)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Apply the Darcy-Weisbach equation to the equivalent pipe.

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->

Use the Darcy-Weisbach equation to calculate the flow rate

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->

Pipe 1

records.content:
A 200 mm pipe with length 3 000 m and f = 0.122 is used in the system below. The end of the pipe is 20 m above the water level. Calculate the energy head that the pump must add to the system to deliver a velocity of 2 m/s? Ignore local losses and losses in the suction pipe. Solve the problem from basic principles.

img = Image.open(r"figures/126.png")
img



Answer


\;393.3 \; m

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The energy head that the pump must add to the system can be calculated from the conservation of energy between the tank and the end of the pipe.

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{align}
&z_1+\frac{p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=z_2+\frac{p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g}+ h_f - h_p \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&v_1 = 0 \text{ (large area)} \\
&p_1 = 0 \text{ (atmospheric)} \\
&z_1 = 0 \text{ (datum)} \\
&p_2 = 0 \text{ (atmospheric)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
& h_p = z_2+\frac{v_2^2}{2g}+h_f\\
&h_f= \frac {fLV^2}{2gD}
\end{align}

%%render long 1
L= 3000 #m
D= 200/1000 #m
f= 0.122 
z_2= 20 #m 
V= 2 #m/s
h_f= f*L*V**2/(2*g*D) #m
h_p= z_2+V**2/(2*g)+h_f #m

records.content:
A pumped supply system will be used to transport water at 15 °C between two reservoirs with a total head difference of 50 m. The pipes of the system consist of a 600 m long pipe on the suction side of the pump with diameter 200 mm and absolute roughness 0.2 mm; and a 2 km long pipe on the delivery side of the pump with diameter 150 mm and absolute roughness of 0.2 mm. The suction and delivery pipes have total local loss coefficients of 80 and 200 respectively. The pump impeller eye is located 4 m below the
water level in the suction reservoir. The required flowrate in the system is 40 L/s. Determine the following:

a) The working point of the pump to deliver the required flowrate.

b) The classification of the flow in the suction and delivery pipes based the zones defined on the Moody diagram.

c) The minimum input power required by the pump if the pump efficiency is assumed to be 75%.

Answer


a) \;191 \; m \; ; \; 40 \; L/s


b) Both transitional turbulent


c) \;99.8\; kW 

Question 2 (a)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Apply the Energy Equation between the two water tanks.

Then write the equation to obtain the pumping head.

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{align}
&z_1+\frac{p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g} + h_p = z_2+\frac{p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g}+ h_{fs} +h_{ss} +h_{fd} +h_{sd} \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&v_1 = v_2 = 0 \text{ (large area)} \\
&p_1 = p_2 =  0 \text{ (atmospheric)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&h_p=\Delta z +h_{fs}+h_{ss}+h_{fd}+h_{sd}
\end{align}

%%render long 6

T=15 #°C
Delta_z=50 #m
L_s=600 #m
D_s=200/1000 #m
e_s=0.2/1000 #m
L_d=2000 #m
D_d=150/1000 #m
e_d=0.2/1000 #m
k_s=80
k_d=200
Q=40/1000 #m3/s
nu=1.14*10**-6 #m2/s


v_s= Q/(pi*D_s**2/4) #m/s

Re_s= v_s*D_s/nu
f_s= (1/(-1.8*log10((e_s/(3.7*D_s))**1.11+(6.9/Re_s))))**2
h_fs= (f_s*L_s*v_s**2)/(2*g*D_s) #m
h_ss= k_s*(v_s**2/(2*g)) #m

v_d= Q/(pi*D_d**2/4) #m/s
Re_d= v_d*D_d/nu
f_d= (1/(-1.8*log10((e_d/(3.7*D_d))**1.11+(6.9/Re_d))))**2
h_fd= (f_d*L_d*v_d**2)/(2*g*D_d) #m
h_sd= k_d*(v_d**2/(2*g)) #m

h_p= Delta_z+h_fs+h_ss+h_fd+h_sd #m



 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->

The working point of the pump is:Q=40 L/s\\
h_p=191 m

Question 2 (b)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Plot Reynolds number and friction factor f on the Moody diagram to identify the flow regime.

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->

From Moody diagram: both flows are transitional turbulent.

Question 2 (c)

Solution

%%render long 2
eta=0.75
P= ((rho_water*g*h_p*Q)/eta)/1000 #kW

records.content:
A pumped supply system will be used to transport water at 15 °C between two reservoirs with a total head difference of 50 m. The system consists of a 2 km long pipe with diameter 150 mm and absolute roughness 0.2 mm. The pipe has a total secondary loss coefficient of 200. The required flow rate in the system is 50 L/s. Determine the following for the system (work from basic principles):

a) The working point of the pump to deliver the required flow rate.

b) The minimum input power required by the pump if the pump efficiency is assumed to be 75%.

Answer


a) \;250.59 \; m 


b) \;163.89\; kW

Question 3 (a)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Apply the conservation of energy between the two reservoirs, including the minor and friction losses and the pump height.

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{align}
&z_1+\frac{p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}= z_2+\frac{p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g}+ h_f + h_m - h_p \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&v_1 = v_2 = 0 \text{ (large area)} \\
&p_1 = p_2 =  0 \text{ (atmospheric)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&h_p=\Delta z + h_f + h_m \\
\end{align}

Note: the viscosity of water at 15°C is \nu=1.139x10^{-6} m^2/s 

%%render long 3
z= 50#m 
L= 2000 #m
D= 150/1000 #mm 
e= 0.2/1000 #m 
k= 200 
Q= 50#L/s
Q_m =Q/1000 #m3/s
eta= 0.75 
V= Q_m*4/(pi*D**2)#m/s
Re= V*D/nu
f= (1/(-2*log10(5.74/(Re**0.9)+(e/(3.7*D)))))**2
h_p= z+f*L*V**2/(2*g*D)+k*V**2/(2*g) #m



Question 3 (b)

Solution

%%render long 2
P= rho_water*g*h_p*Q_m/eta #W
P_= P/1000 #kW

hierarchy.lvl2: Problems	for	Lecture	25: System	curve	and	pump	operating	point
Problems	for	Lecture	25: System	curve	and	pump	operating	point

records.content:
The tanks, pump and pipelines of the system below have properties as shown. The suction line entrance from the pressure tank is flush and the discharge into the open tank is submerged  If the pump P adds 1.5 kW to the system:

a) Determine the flow rate

b) Find the pressure in the pipe on the suction side of the pump. Ignore local losses.

img = Image.open(r"figures/129.png")
img



Answer


a) \;103 \; L/s


b) \;-49.48 \; kPa

Question 4 (a)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Apply the Energy Equation between points 1 and 3.

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{align}
&z_1+\frac{p_1}{\gamma}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}+ h_p = z_3+\frac{p_3}{\gamma}+\frac{v_3^2}{2g}+ h_{fs} + h_{fd} \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
&h_f= \frac{8 fL Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5} \\
&h_p = \frac{P}{\gamma Q} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&v_1 = v_3 = 0 \text{ (large area)} \\
&p_3 =  0 \text{ (atmospheric)} \\
&z_1 =  0 \text{ (datum)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&\frac{p_1}{\gamma}+\frac{P}{\gamma Q}=z_3+\frac{8f_sL_sQ^2}{\pi^2 gD_s^5}+\frac{8f_dL_dQ^2}{\pi^2 gD_d^5}\\
\end{align}

This equation can be rearranged to calculate the flow rate, resulting in three solutions: two negative and one positive. Since flow rate cannot be negative, the valid solution is the positive value derived from the cubic equation.

%%render long
P=1.5*1000 #W
p_1=35000 #Pa
gamma=8000 #N/m3
z_2=4.5 #m
L_s=15 #m
D_s=150/1000 #m
f_s=0.025
L_d=60 #m
D_d=200/1000 #m
f_d=0.03
z_3=-3 #m

Q=symbols('Q')
expr = (p_1/gamma)+(P/(gamma*Q))-z_3-((8*f_s*L_s*Q**2)/(pi**2*g*D_s**5))-((8*f_d*L_d*Q**2)/(pi**2*g*D_d**5))


Q = solve(expr)


Q #m3/s



%%render long
Q= 0.103 #m3/s



Question 4 (b)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Apply the Energy equation between point 1 and point 2.

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{align}
&z_1+\frac{p_1}{\gamma}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g} = z_3+\frac{p_3}{\gamma}+\frac{v_3^2}{2g}+ h_{fs} \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
&h_f= \frac{8 fL Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&v_1 = 0 \text{ (large area)} \\
&z_1 =  0 \text{ (datum)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&p_2=(\frac{p_1}{\gamma}-z_2-\frac{V_2^2}{2 g}-\frac{8f_sL_sQ^2}{\pi^2 gD_s^5})\gamma
\end{align}

%%render long 2
v_2= Q/(pi*(D_s**2/4)) #m/s
p_2= (p_1/gamma-z_2-v_2**2/(2*g)-((8*f_s*L_s*Q**2)/(pi**2*g*D_s**5)))*gamma #Pa
p_2_= p_2/1000 #kPa

records.content:
A pump is installed at 1000 m (patm = 90 kPa) above sea level where the maximum water temperature is estimated to be 20°C (pvap = 2.3 kPA). The pump eye is 2 m above the suction water level. The suction pipe has a velocity of 2 m/s, a friction headloss of 1.2 m and a minor loss of 0.5 m. Calculate the NPSH available for the pump.

Answer


5.24 m

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Set up the energy equation between the suction reservoir and pump inlet. Then calculate the absolute pressure at the pump’s inlet. Finally, calculate the Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH), available using the formula.

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{align}
&z_1+\frac {p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{V_1^2}{2g}=z_2+\frac {p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{V_2^2}{2g}+h_{fs}+h_{ss} \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&v_1 = 0 \text{ (large area)} \\
&z_1 =  0 \text{ (datum)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&\frac {p_1}{\rho g}=z_2+\frac {p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{V_2^2}{2g}+h_{fs}+h_{ss}
\end{align}

Then, NPSH can be calculated as:NPSH_{available}= \frac {p_{abs}-P{vap}}{\rho g} + \frac {V_s^2}{2g}

%%render long 2
p_vap = 2.3*1000 #Pa
V_s= 2 #m/s
h_f= 1.2 #m 
h_m= 0.5 #m 
z_2= 2#m
p_atm = 90*1000 #Pa
p_2= (p_atm/(rho_water*g)-V_s**2/(2*g)-h_f-h_m-z_2)*rho_water*g #Pa
NPSH_available= (p_2-p_vap)/(rho_water*g)+V_s**2/(2*g) #m

records.content:
Consider the figure below. Assume a pipe diameter or 350 mm, f = 0.016 and the lengths of BC = 12 m, DE = 920 m and \Delta z = 48 m. Water at 15°C is pumped from the lower to the upper reservoir. The system is located at altitude of 1000 m above sea level. Point C is 6.0 m above the surface of the lower reservoir. The pump has an NPSHrequired = 2 m. Ignore minor losses and calculate the maximum flow rate that the system will be able to provide without experiencing cavitation.

img = Image.open(r"figures/131.png")
img



Answer


0.575 m^3/s

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The maximum flow rate will occur when cavitation occurs in the pump.

Apply the Energy Equation between the suction reservoir and the pump inlet.

Use NPSH to determine the minimum allowed suction pressure.

Solution

 <!-- ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

-->\begin{align}
&z_A+\frac {p_A}{\rho g}+\frac{V_A^2}{2g} - h_f = z_C+\frac {p_C}{\rho g}+\frac{V_C^2}{2g} \text{  (Conservation of Energy)} \\
&h_f= \frac{fL {V_C}^2}{2 g D} \\
\text{But,   }& \\
&v_A = 0 \text{ (large area)} \\
&z_A =  0 \text{ (datum)} \\
\text{Thus, }& \\
&\frac{p_A}{\rho g}-\frac {fLV_C^2}{2gD}=\frac{p_C}{\rho g}+z_C+\frac{V_C^2}{2 g}\\
\end{align}NPSH_{available}=\frac{p_C}{\rho g}+\frac{V_C^2}{2 g}-\frac{p_v}{\rho g}=2 \hspace{0.2cm} (1)

From the table of Physical properties of water at standard sea-level atmospheric pressure, at T=15°C:\frac{p_C}{\rho g}=0.17 \hspace{0.2cm}m\hspace{0.2cm} abs\\
\frac{p_C}{\rho g}+\frac{V_C^2}{2 g}=2.17 \hspace{0.2cm}m\\

From the able of The ICAO standard atmosphere, at 1000 m above sea level:p_A=89876 \hspace{0.2cm}Pa\\
\frac{p_A}{\rho g}=\frac{89876}{1000*9.81}=9.16\hspace{0.2cm} m\frac{p_A}{\rho g}= 9.16 m \\
z_C= 6 m\\
h_{fAC}= \frac {fLV_C^2}{2gD} = \frac {0.016*12*V_C^2}{2g*0.35}

Replacing this values we have:9.16-\frac {0.016*12*V_C^2}{2g*0.35}=\frac{p_C}{\rho g}+6+\frac{V_C^2}{2 g}\\
\frac{p_C}{\rho g}+\frac{V_C^2}{2 g}= 3.16- 0.549* \frac{V_C^2}{2 g} \hspace{0.2cm} (2)\\

By using equation (2) and substituting it into equation (1), we obtain a system of two equations with two unknowns3.16- 0.549* \frac{V_C^2}{2 g}-0.17=2

Once the velocity is calculated, the flow rate can be determined with the following equation:Q= V_C* A_C

%%render long
D=350/1000 #m
f=0.016
L_BC=12 #m
L_DE=920 #m
dz=48 #m
T=15 #°C
z_C=6 #m
NPSH_required=2 #m
V_C= sqrt((2*g*D)/(f*L_BC)*(9.16-2.16-z_C)) #m/s
A= pi*D**2/4 #m2
Q= A*V_C #m3/s

import handcalcs.render
from sympy import symbols, Eq, solve
from math import sqrt,pi, cos, sin, radians,asin,atan,degrees, tan, log10
from PIL import Image



%%render param
g=9.81 #m/s2
rho_water=1000 #kg/m3
rho_Hg=13600 #kg/m3
p_atm=101325 #Pa

records.content:
Water flows through a sluice gate as shown below. The friction losses through the gate are estimated to be 0.2 m. Find the horizontal thrust of the water per meter width on the sluice gate. Given: y1 = 2.2 m, y2 = 0.4 m, y3 = 0.5 m

img = Image.open(r"figures/132.png")
img= img.resize((300,190))
img



Answer


12.33 \; kN\;to \;the \;right

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Apply the momentum equation in the horizontal direction.

Find the velocities before and after the gate using the Energy equation.

Apply Newton’s Second Law to find the horizontal force.

Solution

img = Image.open(r"figures/25_Momentum_1.png")
img= img.resize((300,190))
img



 ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

Continuity equation:\begin{align*}
Q_1 &= Q_2 \\
v_1 A_1 &= v_2 A_2 \\
v_1(y_1 b) &= v_2(y_2 b) \\
v_1 \frac{y_1}{y_2} &= v_2 \\
\end{align*}

Conservation of energy:\begin{align*}
z_1 + \frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} &= z_2 + \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_f \\
\frac{p_1}{\rho g} &= y_1, \quad \frac{p_2}{\rho g} = y_2 \\
z_1 = z_2 \\
y_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} &= y_2 + \frac{y_1^2 v_1^2}{2g y_2^2} + h_f
\end{align*}

Solving for ( v_1):\begin{align*}
v_1 = \left( \frac{2g(y_2 - y_1 + h_f)}{1 - \frac {y_1^1}{y_2^2}} \right)^{0.5}
\end{align*}

N \mathrm{II}:\begin{align*}
\sum F_x &= \rho Q (v_{2x} - v_{1x}) \\
F_1 - F_2 - R_x &= \rho Q (v_{2x} - v_{1x})
\end{align*}

%%render long 
h_f=0.2 #m
y_1=2.2 #m
y_2=0.4 #m
y_3=0.5 #m
b=1 #m


v_1= ((2*g*(y_2-y_1+h_f))/(1-((y_1**2)/(y_2**2))))**0.5 #m/s
v_2= 5.5*v_1 #m/s
A_1= y_1*b #m2
A_2= y_2*b #m2
Q= v_1*A_1 #m3/s
R_x= rho_water*g*y_1/2*y_1-rho_water*g*y_2/2*y_2-rho_water*Q*(v_2-v_1) #N
R_x_=R_x/1000 #kN



 ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

The force on the structure is the same size but in the opposite direction (N \mathrm{III}）:

%%render 2
F=R_x_ #kN to the right

records.content:
Flow occurs over a spillway with a width of 100 m. The spillway has a cross section as shown below. The water depth on the upstream side of the spillway (y1) is 20 m, and on the downstream side (y2) is 1.5 m. The friction energy headloss over the spillway is 1.2 m. Determine the horizontal force of the water on the spillway.

img = Image.open(r"figures/25_Momentum_2.png")
img= img.resize((300,190))
img



Answer


147.7 \; MN\; to \; the \; right

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Apply the momentum equation in the horizontal direction.

Find the velocities using the Energy equation.

Apply Newton’s Second Law to find the horizontal force.

Solution

img = Image.open(r"figures/25_Momentum_2_2.png")
img= img.resize((300,190))
img



 ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

Conservation of mass:Q_1 = Q_2 \\
v_1 A_1 = v_2 A_2 \\
v_1 y_1 b = v_2 y_2 b \\
v_2 = \frac{y_1 v_1}{y_2}

Conservation of Energy:z_1 + \frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = z_2 + \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_f \\
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} = y_1, \quad \frac{P_2}{\rho g} = y_2 \\
z_1 = z_2\frac{v_1^2}{2g} = y_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_f - y_1 \\
\frac{v_1^2}{2g} = y_2 + \frac{y_1^2 v_1^2}{2g y_2^2} + h_f - y_1

Once the velocities at both sections are determined, the conservation of momentum can then be applied:\sum F_x = \rho Q (v_2 - v_1) \\
F_1 - F_2 -R_x = \rho Q (v_2 - v_1)

The centroid of the spillway is:\rho g \frac{y_1}{2} A_1 - \rho g \frac{y_2}{2} A_2 - F = \rho Q (v_2 - v_1)

%%render long 
b= 100#m 
y_1= 20 #m 
y_2= 1.5 #m 
h_f= 1.2 #m 


v_1= (((y_1-y_2-h_f)*2*g)/((y_1/y_2)**2-1))**0.5 #m/s
v_2= y_1*v_1/y_2#m/s
Q= v_1*y_1*b #m3/s
A_1= b*y_1 #m2
A_2= b*y_2 #m2
R_x= rho_water*g*y_1/2*A_1-rho_water*g*y_2/2*A_2-rho_water*Q*(v_2-v_1) #N
R_xMN=R_x/1000000 #MN



 ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

The force on the structure is on the same site but opposite in direction (N \mathrm{III}）: F_x = 147.735 MN to the right.

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Solution

img = Image.open(r"figures/25_Momentum_3.png")

 ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

Conservation of mass:Q_1=Q_2\\
v_1A_1=v_2A_2\\
v_1(y_1 b_1)=v_2(y_2 b_2)\\
v_2=v_1\frac{y_1 b_1}{y_2 b_2}

Conservation of energy:z_1+\frac {P_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=z_2+\frac {P_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g}\\
\frac {P_1}{\rho g}= y_1, 
\frac {P_2}{\rho g}=y_2\\

y_1+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=y_2+\frac{v_2^2}{2g}\\\begin{align*}
y_1+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=y_2+\frac{(v_1\frac{y_1 b_1}{y_2 b_2})^2}{2g}\\
v_1 = \left( \frac {2g (y_2 - y_1)}{1 - \frac {y_1^2 b_1^2}{y_2^2 b_2^2}} \right) ^{0.5}\\
\end{align*}

N \mathrm{II}:$$\begin{align}
\sum F_x= \rho Q (v_{2x}-v_{1x})\\
F_1-F_2-R_x=\rho Q (v_{2x}-v_{1x})
\end{align}

$$

%%render long 
b_1= 3 #m
b_2= 2.5 #m

y_1= 2 #m
y_2= 1 #m




from sympy.solvers import solve

A_1= b_1*y_1 #m2
A_2= b_2*y_2 #m2

The diameters in the figure below are 750 mm and 500 mm. The pressure at point 1 is 650 kPa and the velocity 2.8 m/s. Neglecting friction, find the resultant force on the horizontal conical reducer if water flows:

Answer


a) \;158.3 \; kN\;to\;the\;right


b) \;158.3 \; kN\;to\;the\;right


c) \;increase \; Fx\;in\;(a)\;and\;decrease\;Fx\;in\;(b)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Apply the conservation of mass to relate the flow rates at different sections and solve for the unknow velocity.

Apply the energy equation.

Apply N \mathrm{II} in the horizontal direction to calculate the force on the reducer.

Question 2 (a)

Solution

 ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

v_1A_1=v_2A_2\\

z_2=0\\

records.content:
A 90° bend bend of a 300 mm diameter pipe lies in the horizontal plane. Water flows through the pipe  at a velocity of 3 m/s and the inlet of the bend has a pressure of 50 kPa.  Neglecting friction losses, calculate the resultant force of the water on the bend.

img = Image.open(r"figures/30_Momentum_1.png")
img= img.resize((600,450))
img



Answer


5.9 \; kN\

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Use conservation of energy (Bernoulli equation) to find the exit pressure.

Apply conservation of momentum in the x and z directions to calculate the force components on the bend.

Solution

img = Image.open(r"figures/30_Momentum_1s.png")
img= img.resize((600,450))
img



 ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

Conservation of energy:z_1+\frac {p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=z_2+\frac {p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g}\\\begin{align*}
\text{but} \quad z_1 &= z_2 \quad;\quad \text{bend in the horizontal plane}\\
v_1 &= v_2 \quad; \quad \text{Conservation of mass}\\
\text{thus} \quad p_1 &= p_2
\end{align*}

Conservation of momentum in x:\sum F_x = \rho Q (v_{2x}-v_{1x})\\R_x-p_2A_2 = \rho Q (v_{2x}-v_{1x})\\

Conservation of momentum in z:\sum F_z = \rho Q (v_{2z}-v_{1z})\\p_1A_1-R_z = \rho Q (v_{2z}-v_{1z})\\

%%render long
v_1= 3 #m/s
p_1=50*1000 #Pa
D=300/1000 #m
v_2 = v_1 #m/s|
A= (pi*D**2)/4 #m2
Q= v_1*A #m3/s
p_2= p_1 #Pa
R_x= p_2*A+rho_water*Q*(v_2-0) #N
R_z= p_1*A-rho_water*Q*(0-v_1) #N
R= sqrt(R_x**2+R_z**2)#N



The force of the water on the bend is the same size, but opposite in direction.

records.content:
A reducing right-angled bend lies in the horizontal plane. Water enters from the west at a velocity of 3 m/s and a pressure of 50 kPa, and leaves toward the north. The diameter of the entrance is 500 mm and at the exit 250 mm. Neglecting friction losses, find the magnitude of the x and y components of the resultant force on the bend.

Answer


11.6 \; kN\;;\;6.2\;kN

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Use conservation of energy (Bernoulli equation) to find the exit pressure.

Apply conservation of momentum in the x and z directions to calculate the force components on the bend.

Solution

img = Image.open(r"figures/30_Momentum_2s.png")
img= img.resize((600,450))
img



 ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

Conservation of energy:\begin{align*}
z_1+\frac {p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g} &= z_2+\frac {p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g}\\
\text{but} \quad z_1 &= z_2 \quad ; \quad \text{horizontal plane}\\
\frac {p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g} &= \frac {p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g}\\
p_2 &= (\frac {p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}-\frac{v_2^2}{2g}) \rho g
\end{align*}

Conservation of momentum in x:\sum F_x = \rho Q (v_{2x}-v_{1x})\\
p_1A_1-R_x = \rho Q (v_{2x}-v_{1x})\\

Conservation of momentum in z:\sum F_z = \rho Q (v_{2z}-v_{1z})\\
R_z-p_2A_2 = \rho Q (v_{2z}-v_{1z})\\

%%render long
v_1= 3 #m/s
p_1= 50*1000 #Pa
D_1= 500/1000 #m
D_2= 250/1000 #m

A_1= pi*D_1**2/4 #m2
A_2= pi*D_2**2/4 #m2
Q= v_1*A_1 #m3/s
v_2= Q/A_2 #m/s
p_2= ((p_1/(rho_water*g))+(v_1**2/(2*g))-(v_2**2/(2*g)))*rho_water*g #Pa
R_x= p_1*A_1-rho_water*Q*(0-v_1) #N
R_z= rho_water*Q*(v_2-0)+p_2*A_2 #N



 ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

The force on the bend in the x direction is 11.584 kN to the right and the force in y direction is 6.2 kN down. (NⅢ）

records.content:
A perforated plate with 50% of its area consisting of small holes is installed at the outlet of a 30 cm diameter horizontal water pipe carrying water at a velocity of 2 m/s. Neglect energy losses and calculate the force of the water on the perforated plate. Assume that the pressure head in the pipe is much greater than it’s diameter, so that you can use a uniform pressure distribution for any pipe cross section.

img = Image.open(r"figures/30_Momentum_3.png")
img= img.resize((600,380))
img



Answer


142 \; N\;to\;the\;right

Use conservation of mass to find the water velocity through the holes.

Apply the Bernoulli euqation to find the pressure difference.

Use conservation of momentum to calculate the force on the perforated plate.

Solution

img = Image.open(r"figures/30_Momentum_3s.png")
img= img.resize((600,380))
img



 ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

Conservation of mass:Q_1=Q_2\\
v_1 A_1=v_2 A_2\\

where A_2 = 0.5 A_1 .v_1 A_1= v_2 \cdot 0.5\cdot A_1\\
v_2=2 v_1

Conservation of energy:z_1+\frac {p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=z_2+\frac {p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g}\\\begin{align*}
\text{where} \quad z_1 &= z_2 \quad ; \quad \text{horizontal plane}\\
p_2 &= 0 \quad ; \quad \text{free jet}
\end{align*}\frac {p_1}{\rho g}=\frac{v_2^2}{2g}-\frac{v_1^2}{2g}

NⅡ:\sum F_x = \rho Q (v_{2x}-v_{1x})\\
p_1 A_1 - R_x = \rho Q (v_{2x}-v_{1x})\\

%%render long 
v_1= 2#m/s
D_1= 30/100 #m

v_2 = v_1/0.5 #m/s
A_1= pi*D_1**2/4
Q= v_1*A_1  #m3/s
P_1= (v_2**2/(2*g)-v_1**2/(2*g))*g*rho_water #Pa
R_x= P_1*A_1-rho_water*Q*(v_2-v_1) #N



 ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

The force on the plate is 141.4 N to the right.(NⅡ)

records.content:
The figure below shows a pipe that ends in a set of two nozzles (points 2 and 3) at right angles to each other, and exiting into the atmosphere. The nozzles are in the horizontal plane. The pipe has a diameter of 150 mm and nozzles 2 and 3 have exit diameters of 100 and 50 mm respectively. The exit velocity at both points 2 and 3 is 10 m/s. Assume ideal flow.Calculate the following:

a)	The flow rate and velocity at at point 1.

b)	The pressure at point 1.

c)	The size and direction of the force that the fluid exerts on the pipe.

d)	Assume the pressure at point 1 remains as in (b), but an energy headloss of 0.5 m occurs through each of the nozzles at points 2 and 3. Calculate the flow rate in the system and the velocities at points 1 and 2. Ignore energy losses other that those at the nozzles and assume the distribution of the flow between 2 and 3 remains the same as in (a).

img = Image.open(r"figures/139.png")
img



Answer


a) \;5.55 \; m/s


b) \;34.57 \; kPa


c) \;420 \; N\; at\; 27.9\; °\; above\; East


d) \;91 \;L/s\;;\; 5.15\; m/s\;;\; 9.3 \;m/s

Question 4 (a)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

To calculate the flow rate and velocity in point 1 the conservation of mass can be used:Q_1=Q_2+Q_3v_1A_1=v_2A_2+v_3A_3

Solution

%%render long 3
D_1= 150/1000 #m 
D_2= 100/1000 #m
D_3= 50/1000 #m 
v_2= 10#m/s
v_3= 10#m/s

A_2= pi*D_2**2/4 #m2
A_3= pi*D_3**2/4#m2
A_1= pi*D_1**2/4 #m2
Q_2= v_2*A_2 #m3/s
Q_3= v_3*A_3 #m3/s
Q_1= Q_2+Q_3 #m3/s
v_1= Q_1/A_1 #m/s



Question 4 (b)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Using conservation of energy:z_1+\frac {p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=z_2+\frac {p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g}\\

z_1=z_2 because is horizontal and p_2=0 Pa because of atmospheric pressure

Considering the assumptions, the pressure in point 1 is:\frac {p_1}{\rho g}=\frac{v_2^2}{2g}-\frac{v_1^2}{2g}\\

Solution

%%render long 2
p_1= ((v_2**2-v_1**2)/(2*g))*rho_water*g #Pa
p_1_= p_1/1000 #kPa



Question 4 (c)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Momentum in x:\sum F_x= \rho Q(v_{out_x}-v_{in_x})\\
\sum F_x= \rho Q(v_2-v_1)\\
p_1A_1-F_x= \rho Q(v_2-v_1)\\
p_1A_1-F_x= \rho Q_2v_2-\rho Q_1v_1)\\

Momentum in y. It is important to include the velocity sign in this equation; in this example v_{3y} is negative.\sum F_y= \rho Q(v_{out_y}-v_{in_y})\\
\sum F_y= \rho Q(-v_3-0)\\
F_3+F_y= \rho Q_3*(-v_3)-0)\\

Once the F_x and F_y are calculated, the force can be determine as:F= \sqrt{F_x^2+F_y^2}

and the direction of the force can be calculated as:\theta= arctan (\frac{F_y}{F_x})

img = Image.open(r"figures/139s.png")
img



Solution

%%render long 2
F_x= p_1*A_1-rho_water*Q_2*v_2+rho_water*Q_1*v_1 #N
F_y= rho_water*Q_3*-v_3#N
F= sqrt(F_x**2+F_y**2) #N
theta= degrees(atan(abs(F_y)/F_x))



Question 4 (d)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Using conservation of energy:z_1+\frac {p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=z_2+\frac {p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g}+h_f\\

z_1=z_2 because is horizontal and p_2=0 because of atmospheric pressure

Assuming the distribution of the flow between 2 and 3 remains the same as in (a), it is necessary to calculate the distributionQ_2= \frac {0.0789}{0.0981}\\Q_2=0.80 Q_1\\Q_3=0.2 Q_1\\v_2A_2=0.80v_1A_1\\

Using conservation of mass:v_2 \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4}=0.80 v_1\frac{\pi D_1^2}{4}\\v_2 D_2^2=0.80 v_1 D_1^2\\v_2 (0.1)^2=0.80 v_1 (0.15)^2\\v_2= 1.8 v_1

Using conservation of energy and mass with an energy headloss of 0.5 m, we have:\frac {p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=\frac{(1.8 v_1)^2}{2g}+h_f\\

Solution

%%render long 2
h_f=0.5 #m
ratio_Q= Q_2/Q_1
ratio_v= ratio_Q*D_1**2/D_2**2
v_1= sqrt(((p_1/(rho_water*g)-h_f)*2*g)/(ratio_v**2-1))#m/s
v_2= ratio_v*v_1#m/s
Q= (v_1*A_1)*1000 #L/s

records.content:
A jet is bent through an angle θ = 120° by a blade in the horizontal plane as shown below. The water jet has a velocity of 25 m/s and a diameter of 20 mm. Calculate the size and direction of the force on the blade.

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/142.png")
img



Answer


340 \; N\;at\;30\;°\;above\;the\;horizon

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The jet direction will be parallel to the plate after hitting it.

Assume the jet speed remains constant.

Ignore friction losses.

Apply NⅡ.

Solution

img = Image.open(r"figures/31_Momentum_3.png")
img



 ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

NⅡ in the x direction:\sum F_x= \rho Q(v_{2_x}-v_{1_x})\\
-R_x= \rho Q(v_{2_x}-v_{1_x})\\\begin{align*}
v_1 & = 25 \quad \text{m/s}\\
v_2 &= v_1\\
v_{1x} &= v_1\\
v_{1y} &= 0\\
v_{2x} &= -v_2 \sin \theta\\
v_{2y} &= -v_2 \cos \theta\\
-R_x &= \rho Q (v_{2x}-v_{1x})\\
-R_y &= \rho Q (v_{2y}-v_{1y})\\
\end{align*}

%%render long 4
theta= 120 #°
v=25 #m/s
D=20/1000 #m

A= pi*D**2/4 #m2
Q= v*A #m3/s

alpha= 180-theta #°

R_x= -rho_water*Q*(-v*cos(radians(alpha))-v)#N
R_y= -rho_water*Q*(-v*sin(radians(alpha))-0)#N

F= sqrt(R_x**2+R_y**2) #N

theta_F= degrees(atan(R_y/R_x)) #°
