records.content:
A jet of water with a velocity of 25 m/s and diameter of 40 mm strikes a blade in a horizontal plane. If the angle of deflection is 115° and friction reduces the velocity to 22 m/s, find;

a.	the component of the force acting on the blade in the direction of the jet.

b.	the component of the force normal/ perpendicular to the jet.

c.	the magnitude and direction of the resultant force exerted on the blade.

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/143.png")
img



Answer


a) \;1.077 \; kN\; to\; the\; right


b) \;0.626 \; kN\; downwards


c) \;1.246 \; kN\; at\;30.2\;°\;below\;east

Question 4 (a)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The force on the X-axis can be calculated with the momentum equation as:\sum F_x= \rho Q(v_{out_x}-v_{in_x})\\
-R_x= \rho Q(v_{out_x}-v_{in_x})\\

In this example, the velocity component along the X-axis can be determined as followsv_{2x}= v_2*cos (180-\theta)

In the conservation of momentum, it is important to include the sign that indicates the direction of the velocity component. Therefore, in this exercise, the v_{2x} component is negative-R_x= \rho Q(-v_{2x}-v_{1x})

Solution

%%render long 3
v_1= 25 #m/s
D= 40/1000 #m
theta= 115 #°
theta_rad= radians(180-theta) #rad
v_2= 22 #m/s


A_1= pi*D**2/4 #m2
Q= v_1*A_1 #m3/s
R_x= -rho_water*Q*(-v_2*cos(theta_rad)-v_1) #N



Question 4 (b)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

The force on the Y-axis can be calculated with the momentum equation as:\sum F_y= \rho Q(v_{out_y}-v_{in_y})\\
R_y= \rho Q(v_{out_y}-v_{in_y})\\

In this example, the velocity component along the X-axis can be determined as followsv_{2y}= v_2*sin (180-\theta)R_y= \rho Q(v_{2y}-v_{1y})

In this example, there is no component of the velocitu one in the Y-axis

Solution

%%render long 2
R_y= rho_water*Q*(v_2*sin(theta_rad)-0)



Question 4 (c)

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Once the R_x and R_y are calculated, the force can be determine as:F= \sqrt{R_x^2+R_y^2}

and the direction of the force can be calculated as:\theta= arctan (\frac{R_y}{R_x})

Solution

%%render long 2
F= sqrt (R_x**2+R_y**2) #N
theta_F= degrees(atan(R_y/R_x)) #°

records.content:
A 2 km long, 500 mm diameter pipeline carries water at a velocity of 2 m/s.  Calculate the pressure rise in the system if water assuming a rigid pipe and a water temperature of 15 °Celsius.

Answer


298 \; m

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Calculate the celerity of the transient wave c.

Calculate the pressure increase using the Joukowski equation.

Solution

 ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

To calculate the pressure rise in the system the water hammer equation can be used.c= \sqrt {E_v}{\rho_{water}}\\
\Delta p= \rho c v_o\\
h_t= \frac {\Delta p}{\rho_{water} g}

The bulk modulus of elasticity can be found in Table A.1 for 15°C E_v= 2.14 x10^6 kN/m^2

%%render long 2
D= 500#mm 
L= 2 #km 
v= 2 #m/s

E_v=2.14*10**9 #N/m2
c= sqrt (E_v/(rho_water)) #m/s
Delta_P= rho_water*c*v #Pa 
h_t= Delta_P/ (rho_water*g) #m

records.content:
Calculate the celerity of a pressure wave in water at 20 °C assuming a water density of 1000 kg/m3 . What difference does it make to your answer if change in water density with temperature is taken into account?

Answer


1476.48 \; m/s\; 1477.81\; m/s\; ,\; thus\; less\; than\; 0.1\; \% \; difference

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Calculate the celerity of the transient wave c.

Calculate the pressure increase using the Joukowski equation.

Solution

 ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputsc= \sqrt {\frac{E_v}{\rho}}

From Table A.1, the density of water at 20 °C is 998.2 kg/m^3From Table A.4, E_v= 1030 x10^6 N/m^2

The effect of the density can be calculated with the difference between the celerities:\text{difference} = \left( \frac{c_2 - c_1}{c_2} \right)  100

%%render long 2
T=20 #°C
rho=1000 #kg/m3

rho_20=998.2 #kg/m3
E_v=2.18*10**6*1000 #N/m2
c_1= sqrt(E_v/rho) #m/s
c_2= sqrt(E_v/rho_20) #m/s
difference= ((c_2-c_1)/c_2)*100 #%

records.content:
Find the celerity of a pressure wave in benzene if a rigid pipe is assumed. If the initial flow velocity is 2 m/s, what will the increased pressure and pressure head be if a valve in the pipe is suddenly closed?

Answer


\;1081.88\; m/s\;;\; 1.9\; MPa\;; \;220.57\; m \;benzene

 ```{Dropdown} Hint

Calculate the celerity of the transient wave c.

Calculate the pressure increase using the Joukowski equation.

Solution

 ```{Dropdown} Show code cell outputs

To calculate the pressure rise in the system, the water hammer equation can be used:\begin{align*}
c &= \sqrt{ \frac{E_v}{\rho_{\text{water}}} } \\
\Delta p &= \rho c v
\end{align*}

After calculating the pressure increase, the next step is to determine the pressure head:\begin{align*}
H &= \frac{ \Delta p }{ \rho_{\text{water}} g }
\end{align*}

In Table A.4 for benzene: S = 0.88 and E_v= 1030 x10^6 N/m^2

%%render long 2
V = 2#m/s

S=0.88 
rho_benz= S*rho_water #kg/m3
E_v=1030*10**6 #N/m2
c= sqrt (E_v/(S*rho_water)) #m/s
Delta_P= rho_benz*c*V #Pa 
Delta_P_Mpa= Delta_P/1000000 #MPa
H= Delta_P/ (rho_benz*g) #m

This book contains the exercises for the course, each accompanied by helpful hints and their corresponding solutions.

The exercises are organized by course modules.

Module 1: Fluid properties

Learn the fundamental properties of fluids.

Module 2: Hydrostatics

Exercises related to hydrostatic pressure and forces.

Module 3: Kinematics and Conservation of Mass

Problems on flow kinematics and mass conservation.

Module 4: Conservation of Energy

Apply Bernoulli and energy equations.

Module 5: Conservation of Momentum

Momentum and force balance in fluid systems.
